title: Cramer's Rule and Linear Applications
summary: Solve linear systems using Cramer's Rule, and apply matrix methods to electric circuits.
content_html:
Cramer's Rule

For a system Ax = b where A is n×n and det(A) ≠ 0, the unique solution is:

xᵢ = det(Aᵢ) / det(A)

where Aᵢ is the matrix A with column i replaced by b.

Example — 2×2 System

2x + y = 5    →  A = [2  1],  b = [5]
3x + 4y = 6        [3  4]       [6]

det(A) = 2·4 - 1·3 = 8 - 3 = 5

A₁ = [5  1]  → det(A₁) = 5·4 - 1·6 = 14
     [6  4]
A₂ = [2  5]  → det(A₂) = 2·6 - 5·3 = -3
     [3  6]

x₁ = 14/5,  x₂ = -3/5

Example — 3×3 System

x  + 2y + z  = 4
3x + y  + 2z = 7
2x + 3y + z  = 6

A = [[1,2,1],[3,1,2],[2,3,1]]   det(A) = 1(1-6)-2(3-4)+1(9-2)
                                       = -5+2+7 = 4

A₁ = [[4,2,1],[7,1,2],[6,3,1]]  det(A₁) = 4(1-6)-2(7-12)+1(21-6)
                                          = -20+10+15 = 5

x = det(A₁)/det(A) = 5/4

When to Use Cramer's Rule

Cramer's Rule is efficient for small systems (2×2, 3×3). For larger systems, Gaussian elimination is preferred as computing many determinants becomes expensive.

Application — Electric Circuits (Kirchhoff's Laws)

In a circuit with resistors and voltage sources, apply:

  • KVL: Sum of voltages around any loop = 0.
  • KCL: Sum of currents at any node = 0.

This gives a system of linear equations in terms of loop currents, solvable using matrices.

Loop equations (2 loops, currents I₁, I₂):
R₁I₁ + R₂(I₁-I₂) = V₁   →  (R₁+R₂)I₁ - R₂I₂ = V₁
R₂(I₂-I₁) + R₃I₂ = V₂   →  -R₂I₁ + (R₂+R₃)I₂ = V₂

Write as [R₁+R₂  -R₂  ] [I₁] = [V₁]
         [-R₂   R₂+R₃] [I₂]   [V₂]

Solve using Cramer's Rule or matrix inversion.

exercises.title: Cramer's Rule formula
exercises.prompt: In Cramer's Rule, xᵢ is calculated as:

exercises.title: Aᵢ definition
exercises.prompt: In Cramer's Rule, matrix Aᵢ is formed by:
options: Replacing column i of A with vector b
exercises.answer: Replacing column i of A with vector b

exercises.title: KVL law
options: Sum of voltages around any loop = 0
Sum of currents at a node = 0
exercises.answer: Sum of voltages around any loop = 0

exercises.title: Efficiency of Cramer's
exercises.prompt: For large n×n systems, Cramer's Rule is less preferred because:
options: It only works for square matrices

exercises.title: Unique solution requirement
exercises.prompt: Cramer's Rule gives a unique solution when det(A):

exercises.title: KCL law
options: Sum of currents entering a node = sum leaving
exercises.answer: Sum of currents entering a node = sum leaving

exercises.title: Cramer for 3×3
exercises.prompt: For a 3×3 system with Cramer's Rule, you need to compute:

exercises.title: Matrix form of circuit
exercises.prompt: Why is matrix form useful for circuit analysis?
options: It allows systematic solution of multiple loop current equations
exercises.answer: It allows systematic solution of multiple loop current equations
